> What causes API to stop responding? Is patroni making lots of requests to it?

Every pod is doing 2 read requests (list pods + list endpoints) every 10 seconds + 1 write request (update pod). In addition to that master is doing update leader endpoint write request.
That's not very much for k8s api.

> When API isn't responding why patroni restarts database? Couldn't it just wait until server comes up without doing any action?

When Patroni can't access API, it has no other option rather than demote master to read-only, because it doesn't know what is going on. It could be that API is not accessible on a specific Pod due to network partitioning and meanwhile some other Pod see that there is no master and promotes.

> Should be increase timeout period?

It's possible to increase retry_timeout and ttl by calling `patronictl edit-config` on one of the Pods:
```
$ kubectl exec -ti patronipod-0 bash
# su postgres
$ patronictl edit-config
```

There is one rule you should follow when changing ttl, loop_wait and retry_timeout:
```ttl >= loop_wait + retry_timeout*2```

> is it possible that issue lies within kube-proxy?

It's hard to know for sure, but I think that it's definitely possible.

The second point is clearly very helpful.

The firs one is less clear for me. 

- Users may want to initialize a simulation using many ways. Using a JSON file is one way but you can initialize a simulation using many other inputs. Examples are basic test_case `Scenario` or `SurveyScenario` but you can build many others higher level objects.
So I would favor a separation between the step of simulation creation and simulation initialisation.
That is almost the cas today if you do not use a  `simulation_json` initialisation file since the latter can be used as a parameter at the `Simulation` instantiation.

- I definitively would not favor linking the simulation creation to the `tax_benefit_system`.
The latter is an abstract representation of the legislation. The `Simulation` is handling data and computation and thus need a `tax_benefit_system` to work but not the way around. 
Both could/should be driven by a higher level object (like `Scenario`) that defines how initialisation and computation should be achieved.

- A uniform approach might be using a default `initialize`or `fill` method to inject data after the initialisation of a simulation (mainly instantiation of the simulation and its empty entities). The default one could be the one using the JSON file but that should not prevail on other way of filling in data.

if anyone is curious, this is how I solved it, may be worth adding to the lib as a supported mechanism.

```python
class UnparentedContext(Context):
    def end_subsegment(self, end_time=None):
        """ This differs from the base context in that if we're closing the first subsegment, and the parent
        is an unparented segment, we close the parent. """
        subsegment = self.get_trace_entity()
        if self._is_subsegment(subsegment):
            subsegment.close(end_time)
            self._local.entities.pop()
            root_entity = self._local.entities[0]
            if len(self._local.entities) == 1 and root_entity.name == _UNPARENTED_SEGMENT_NAME:
                xray_recorder.end_segment()

            return True
        else:
            log.warning("No subsegment to end.")
            return False

    def handle_context_missing(self):
        """
        Called whenever there is no trace entity to access or mutate.
        """
        xray_recorder.begin_segment(_UNPARENTED_SEGMENT_NAME)
        return self.get_trace_entity()
```

Also if you had to handle 2 different items in the context it may get interleaved in the middle. m.context['flag1'] += ['a']

m.context['flag2] += ['b']

So you need to use 
with m.locked_context() as global_context:
     global_context['flag1'] += ['a'] 
     global_context['flag2'] += ['b']

I believe this is doing what it should -- as GeoPt isn't something supported by the JSON API, but instead is an abstraction in DB / NDB

- GeoPt data type: https://code.google.com/p/googleappengine/source/browse/trunk/python/google/appengine/api/datastore_types.py?r=503#877

- GeoPtProperty in NDB: https://code.google.com/p/googleappengine/source/browse/trunk/python/google/appengine/ext/ndb/model.py?r=503#1748

So under the hood, it looks like NDB is taking a GeoPt and turning into a pair of coordinates before saving via the API -- and then loading it into that property on the way out of the API as well.

If we get NDB running on top of the Cloud Datastore API this problem should go away as you'd just use NDB the whole time (see to #557).

Hey @frankyn sure thing! Right now with requester pays, let's say that I have a project space bucket called BlueBucket. BlueBucket is tied to a web service where people can browse and download their favorite color files. I put an awesome file there, RedFile, and then [per the documentation](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/requester-pays) if I turn on requester pays, some of the requesters pay for the charges to interact with it. But I'm still paying these things:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/814322/37651992-6099a5da-2c10-11e8-82bd-733a45e8639a.png)
(and this is my reference to "include storage objects themselves")

Now let's say that  many others contribute GreenFile, OrangeFile, and the way this is possible is via my application authenticating and handling the upload. This means that I am logically paying for storage, and the best I can do is have those that want to download / otherwise request GreenFile and OrangeFile to pay for that!  But this gets poblematic (for me) over time because of the costs of RedFile, GreenFile, and OrangeFile are mostly on me.

Instead I would want a distributed cost model. Since the user (let's call him OrangeMan) of OrangeFile is likely to also be the primary requester, I would want to provide him the service if he is responsible for payment of requests to use that file **and** for the storage itself. This way, we have a bunch of files in the same bucket still, available for the service, but the cost is distributed among the users.

The real world scenario is at Stanford (or other academic places) where we are building tools for scientific containers. The containers are huge, and the tools are basic things like a registry. I would want to provide the tool for Stanford, but have labs (each signed up with their own Google project space accounts) to take responsibility for charges for their images.

Does that make sense?

@jsirois @kwlzn I'm struggling with the fix to this - I've found a couple of alternative ways to solve it, none of which I'm really happy with.

The problem, as I understand it:

A simple wheel is just a zip file containing a set of files and directories which include the package source. When those are installed  by pip, depending on the setting of the setting of the Root_Is_Purelib
parameter of the WHEEL metadata, it's installed into either the purelib or platlib directory. Inside of a PEX, we can just unzip one of these directories, and arrange, during the PEX bootstrap, for that directory to get added to the sys.path. (because, once it's in a pex, the distinction between purelib and platlib isn't relevant - whether the root is purelib or platlib, it needs to get added to the
PYTHONPATH.)

Wheels, however, don't always follow this simple structure. When they want to specify stuff to be installed in multiple places (eg, some code in purelib, some in platlib; or some purelib and some headers; etc.), they can include a package.data directory. That contains subdirectories, whose name is the install path element that their contents should be put into. 

For example, we have opencv, which contains a set of data files and a set of purelib source files. So opencv_python-3.2.0.7-cp27-cp27mu-linux_x86_64.whl contains opencv_python-3.2.0.7-cp27-cp27mu-linux_x86_64.data, which has two subdirectories - data (containing some text files) and purelib (containing the actual python modules).

In a wheel with a data thing, the pex trick of just adding the wheel root to the sys.path doesn't work. For example, in opencv, we'd end up adding the wheel location .deps/opencv_python-3.2.0.7-cp27-cp27mu-linux_x86_64 to the sys.path, but the python modules are actually in .deps/opencv_python-...-x86_64.data/purelib.  

In fact, we really need to get the contents of all of the .data directory subdirectories into a place where the python interpreter needs to load them. Fortunately, in the pex world, we don't really need to maintain the distinction between the different instal_path components - they're all just things that need to wind up on the sys.path for loading.

John's initial comment suggested that they way to handle this is to take advantage of the fact
that a wheel knows how to install itself: we can create a WheelFile, and use it's install
method, with the target locations for the different install path elements overridden appropriately
for a pex. 

If I'm understanding John's suggestion correctly, to make this work, we'd need to create new directories in the pex corresponding to the install_path components, and then tell the wheel  to install itself into those directories. (Basically, we'd be creating something that looks like sitelibs into a pex - instead of .deps/opencv...whl/ containing the contents of opencv, we'd have a .deps/sitelibs, which contains all of the python module sources for things in the pex.)  This is the cleanest solution, in terms of using wheels the way they're intended, but it would be a pretty substantial change to the internal structure of a pex.

Alternatively, we can leave the pexes as is, and change the way that the pex bootstrap computes
the sys.path when it runs a pex, so that if a wheel contains a .data dir, we add
each of its components to the sys.path. This feels much uglier, but it wouldn't perturb the way that pexes currently operate.

I've got a working prototype of the latter approach, for which I'm
working on tests. But before I spend too much more time banging away at it, I'd
like to verify that (a) the stuff I figured out above is correct, and (2) the
sys.path modification approach is something that is acceptable to the PEX maintainers.

> We also need to decide whether we merge the resulting pex files using PEX_PATH or something similar to the pex merging API from ResolveRequirementsTaskBase.merged_pex.

`PEX_PATH` is effectively a build-time overhead optimization. in the case of e.g. `./pants test` this permits us to create two primary chained pex files: a sources pex and a 3rdparty pex, such that when you modify only local sources we can cheaply rebuild the sources pex sans a resolve and quickly recompose the two halves for test execution.

unless I'm mistaken, I believe the use of `merged_pex` as a replacement for `PEX_PATH` would result in a 3rdparty resolve on each use - which would slow things down. this merge & re-resolve is strictly required for `./pants binary`, since we have to output exactly one on-disk product for that. for `test`, `run` and `repl` we should likely continue to use `PEX_PATH` if possible tho.

RemoteSources target has a field called "dest" which is the destination target type. In v2 engine, during parsing, all target type is converted to TargetAdaptor (including JvmAppAdaptor and PythonTargetAdaptor). Thus "dest" field is converted to TargetAdaptor as well. When instantiating TargetAdaptor to normal target type, "dest" field is not converted, thus trigger exception.

A warning from our (limited) experience with this work: it seems like folks who fill out forms are more likely to include extraneous and undocumented/informal information in fields for organization/corporate names than they are for fields about people's name. The formal parser routine is pretty good and straightforward for parsing cleanish corporate names. The difficulty we ran into was understanding things like "Corp A on behalf of Corp B" or "Corp A formerly known as Corp B". Although only a fourth of the file you linked deals with embedded relationships, the vast amount of work I put in on the parser as a whole was dealing with how to represent and deal with the information from embedded relationships. We've only run this on one dataset, so who knows if the pattern holds though. I guess the main point is that in our experience the company names have been messier than people's name for parsing purposes.

Yes, this broke neutron-api charm.

The current version of the code detects differences in the cert files at every check (update-status every 2min) and re-writes the file.
SSL validations will fail while the file is re-written.

I didn't use the version from charmstore. But as far as i know it is released that way. 
The py3 fixes in neutron-api are from November.
I raised it here because I assume it will affect any charm that uses it.

Easy test

t: GET /v2.0/ports.json?device_id=6927fe06-920d-478c-b4dd-b8983ab2c5e8 HTTP/1.0
2017-12-18 16:37:52 INFO juju-log Installing new CA cert

 HTTP/1.0
```
after

unit-neutron-api-1: 11:24:31 INFO unit.neutron-api/1.juju-log CA cert is the same as installed version
```

old_cert = retrieve_ca_cert(cert_file) reads a normal string
But ca_cert is read in binary mode. (correctly)
old_cert and ca_cert will not be equal even if the string is the same.


This is how I dealt with it. Just read the file in binary mode in retrieve_ca_cert()

```
--- a/hooks/charmhelpers/contrib/hahelpers/apache.py
+++ b/hooks/charmhelpers/contrib/hahelpers/apache.py
@@ -76,7 +76,7 @@ def get_ca_cert():
 def retrieve_ca_cert(cert_file):
     cert = None
     if os.path.isfile(cert_file):
-        with open(cert_file, 'rb') as crt:
+        with open(cert_file, 'r') as crt:
             cert = crt.read()
     return cert
```

I looked into the bundle_manager.py code that handles job to worker assignments, but I don't think I have the time to work on an acceptable pull request.

For future reference, as far as I can tell, it would require looking at all running jobs on each worker to determine whether or not the requested resource is exhausted in the function `_filter_and_sort_workers`. This filtering should probably be performed after all other filtering, because other filtering requires only pre-loaded information. The filtering could proceed as follows:
* Generate a mapping from run_uuids -> workers using `worker.run_uuids` for each `worker` in `workers_list`
* Make a deep copy of `workers_list` to prevent modifying values in the WorkerInfoAccessor
* Obtain list of `running_bundles` by calling `self._model.batch_get_bundles(state=State.RUNNING, bundle_type='run')`
* For each `bundle` in `running_bundles`
  * Look up corresponding worker using uuid
  * For each exhaustable resource used by the bundle (i.e. gpu, memory)
    * Decrement the corresponding value in worker by the amount used by the bundle
* Perform original filtering again

Though the above is all based on my own understanding, so there are probably things that should be tweaked, or might be completely incorrect.

I may have contributed to create a bit of confusion around this float strings issue. Let me try to clarify it again.

The problem I see in the ufonormalizer has not to do with the difference between python 2 and 3's `str` vs `repr` of floats. The `xmlConvertFloat` currently uses explicit string formatting with a fixed number of decimal digits (`"%.10f" % value`). This of course always produces identical results in both python 2 and 3.

That was indeed an issue in ufoLib, since the latter was using the `str` built-in to stringify floats, and on Python2 `str` only displays 12 [significant digits](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Significant_figures), whereas `repr` works the same way across Python 2 and 3 (i.e. uses up to 17 significant digits, but prefers the shortest representation among equivalent ones).

The issue I'm trying to solve here, by replacing a fixed number of decimal digits with `repr`, is that of _roundtripping_ float => str => float. I do agree with you that being fonts designed on a coarse grid the difference between 0.0000000001 and 0.00000000005 is negligible from a design point of view; and that UFO file size would get bigger if we increased the float strings precision.

However, I think it should be left to the user to decide whether and how much to round floats. I believe that there are situations when you don't want to have *any* rounding, e.g. when you want to ensure that conditions that hold before floats have been serialized to a .glif file will still hold by the time these float strings are converted back to float numbers upon reading the file.

The built-in `repr` uses the maximum number of "significant" digits (which are not the same thing as the number of digits after the decimal point) that are required to ensure that `eval(repr(f)) == f` -- while also choosing the shortest representation among the possible ways of representing a binary double-precision floating point as a decimal number.

The UFO spec (rightly) leaves the door open to float numbers of arbitrary precision. The choice of 10 decimal positions, while I agree it's sensible and reasonable, it's still arbitrary nonetheless.
I think it'd be preferable if the ufonormalizer did not do any rounding of floats, and simply ensured that they maintain their original value as they are written out as strings and read back in. Alternatively, the rounding could be made into option of the ufonormalizer script, if desired.

For MIME types, "text" is treated as a type rather than a format. In python, these terms have been muddled between v2 and v3, but the six library created a convention that "text" refers to a sequence of characters (i.e. a unicode sequence), and "string" refers to a sequence of bytes, which may encode text. That usage of "string" probably emerged from the C kingdom, where strings are sequences of bytes.

Not sure exactly what naming convention we should adopt, but we should probably document what we mean with this type one way or the other.

James, the problem was only indirectly related to MutatorMath. The main problem was that you forgot to configure logging; you were relying on the "last resort" handler: i.e. the one which outputs to stderr when the user does not explicitly set up any handler for the root logger (and only if log messages level is >= WARNING). On the other hand, MutatorMath is calling basicConfig (whereas it shouldn't, TL;DR see below); hence, the last-resort stderr handler is not used when fontmake uses mutatorMath, and since you did not configure it, you see no messages from your timer logger.

You should fix this by simply calling `logging.basicConfig` yourself from within fontmake's `main()` function.
I suggest that you decrease the level of the timer's logger to "DEBUG", as "WARNING" is too high for this sort of information.
Here's the patch that solves this particular fontmake issue.


Now, the way MutatorMath uses logging is rather uncommon for a library.
It is usually the user of the a library who's responsible to configure any loggers (e.g. attaching a file or stream handler, with optionally some format strings and verbosity level, etc.), not the library itself.

The logging configuration should be done inside a script's main() function. However, MutatorMath was calling the `basicConfig` function from the constructor of `DesignSpaceDocumentReader`, which fontmake uses. The problem is that configures the handler for the "root" logger, which is the parent of all the loggers; so it has an effect not only on MutatorMath's logger, but on any other library which happen to use `logging` module. I'm going to send a PR for this specific issue to the upstream MutatorMath repo.

Looking at this issue again, I think I understand why the RAM usage is so high. We have a code that eliminate duplicate maxima after the `find_maxima`. These occur in the case of flat peaks, with multiple pixels having the maximum intensity. This is OK for small features in greyscale images, but less so for binary images.  These will have R^2 equally high feature pixels, providing order (R^2)! overlapping features that are filtered out right after `find maxima`.

We need a warning for binary images, especially when large `diameter` is used. I guess that the grey dilation technique that we use is not suitable for your images.

Originally I thought there would only be one `SMCService` running (possibly as a `NotarySMCService`) and a `ProposerService` would just have a reference ot the `SMCHandler` to call something like `propose`. ```
```
Another direction is that in the earlier draft design, there is a new ShardMonitor process that is responsible to poll SMC and notify ShardManager that manages multiple shards. ShardManager is just a very abstract idea by that time, it can be considered as the middle layer between ShardServer and ShardSyncer. The sub-services (NotaryService, ProposerService) of different shards can have a pubsub relationship with ShardMonitor.
```

`ShardMonitor` seems pretty close to what I envision `SMCService` to be. However, I don't see value in adding a `ShardManager` as another layer between `SMCService` and `ShardSyncer`. By default `mypy` doesn't let us access attributes that aren't defined which is a good safety check to have. But it doesn't seem to recognize attributes defined via `slots` (leaving out for a moment that the string list donation doesn't let us specify the type of the property).

```python
class MyThing:
    __slots__ = ["prop_a"]

y = MyThing()
y.prop_a = 'test'
```

Leaves you with

```shell
$ mypy slots.py 
slots.py:5: error: "MyThing" has no attribute "prop_a"
```

Whereas the following works fine

```python
class MyThing:
    prop_a: str

y = MyThing()
y.prop_a = 'test'
```

**Plus**, it has the benefit of catching type bugs. E.g

```python
class MyThing:
    prop_a: str

y = MyThing()
y.prop_a = 4
```

```shell
$ mypy slots.py 
slots.py:14: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "int", variable has type "str")
```

>It looks like if we instead use __slots__ we could see 40-50% reduction in memory footprint.

I admit that I didn't really dive into *why* the `slots` construct would reduce the memory footprint that much but if it comes from the fact that we are pre-initializing all these attributes to `None` then I believe this won't be an issue with Python 3.6 anymore because there you don't need to assign `None` (or any other value) to an attribute just to denote it. You can simply declare it with its type and as far as I think this won't have any effect on runtime performance but be simply a hint for static analyzers such as `mypy`.

```python
class MyThing:
    prop_a: str
```

That's one downside of Python 3.5 that is also explicitly mentioned in [PEP526](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0526/).

>There's no way to annotate the type of an undefined variable; one needs to initialize it to None (e.g. a = None # type: int).

OK, turning `_` into a separator is easy. However, it kills my suggested solution for openssl to replace `1.0.2` with `1.0.2_`. To solve this problem, one could allow string components to start with `@`, to the effect that these strings are considered greater than numbers, i.e.

    1.2.3dev4 < 1.2.3a1 < 1.2.3 < 1.2.3@a1 < 1.2.3post1

openssl versions would thus be replaced according to `1.0.2a  =>  1.0.2@a`. The letter `@` (meaning 'after' in this context) is one of the few characters in the ASCII set that can be used here without creating conflicts in either version string analysis or the shell (alternatives are `%`, `~` and perhaps `$`; I also considered `^` but it can't be used because it is the DOS shell's escape character). This proposal is also compatible with PEP 440.

OK, I find myself softening to the idea of linking them together.

I do think we need to do a better job of making `conda`'s references to Anaconda more generic. We can figure out how to do that. We need `conda` to behave sensibly if `anaconda-client` is not installed. But I think we should define a very plugin API that `conda` could use for search functionality and perhaps even the token-based authentication. `anaconda-client` would then be but an *instance* of this plugin. 

With this in mind, it may indeed make sense for `anaconda-client` to be installed whenever `conda` is installed from anaconda.org. If a third party implemented their own conda server, _they_ could configure their `conda` dependencies to automatically pull down _their_ client.

If the plugin API were properly designed, `conda` could interact with multiple instances of these API plugins, supporting search and authentication across multiple servers.

> It appears that starting in conda 4.4.* environments listed in ~/.conda/environments.txt are listed when doing "conda env list"

Yes, this is true.

> although they cannot be entered or deleted unless they were created by the conda installation in use

I don't believe this to be true.  Especially when you have multiple versions of conda floating around, it's important to think of environments having *locations* rather than environments just having *names*.  The implication is that, when addressing an environment, use the `-p` / `--prefix` flag rather than the `-n` / `--name` flag, or in the case of `conda activate`, use the full path to the environment.  The first column of `conda env list` (or `conda info -e`) is the environment *name*, whereas the second column is the *location*.  If an environment name isn't listed, it'll have to be referenced using the environment location.

> I'm not even sure if multiple conda installations interact with ~/.conda/ contents in a concurrency safe manner?

We don't have any locking in conda right now.  So accessing `~/.conda/environments.txt` is the least of my worries for having multiple conda processes running at the same time.  Tracking issue for that is https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6742.

> Since conda is primarily concerned with providing isolated environments, I would expect there to be a way for conda itself to be limited to an environment or filesystem location. If not the default, I would hope that this would at least be possible via some installation/base-environment specific setting.

You didn't include the output of `conda config --show-sources` (or an exemplary output in your case).  Are you using default configuration across the board?  If so, and if the package cache directory and envs directory in the base environment aren't writable, conda by default will fall back to the `~/.conda` directory for both of those as well.

I don't think I've yet fully appreciated all your concerns here, but I'd like to.  So let's keep the dialogue going.

I do want to point out the *reason* for us recording environment location in `~/.conda/environments.txt`, and the reason (at least so far) we haven't made that configurable.  Running `conda info --envs` as UID 0 will list *every* known conda environment on your system.  It'll use `/etc/passwd` to get home directories for users, and then check for the `~/.conda/environments.txt` file.  Extending that further, running something like `conda search --envs 'openssl<1.0.2o'` will show every outdated conda-installed version of opennssl on the whole system, and the environment location of the environment it's installed in.

Hi,

I noticed that the first error I got was a JSONdecode error:

```
DEBUG conda.core.package_cache:_make_single_record(284): unable to read /home/fullam/miniconda3/pkgs/qt-5.6.2-5/info/repodata_record.json

```

Whic was then followed by the FileNotFound error for every package after that.
```
DEBUG conda.core.package_cache:_make_single_record(283): unable to read /home/fullam/miniconda3/pkgs/ipython-6.2.1-py36_0/info/repodata_record.json
  because FileNotFoundError(2, 'No such file or directory')
```

I looked into it a bit:
Error was occuring in package_cache.py in  _make_single_record.
specifically the line:

```
  package_cache_record = PackageCacheRecord.from_objects(
                repodata_record,
                package_tarball_full_path=package_tarball_full_path,
                extracted_package_dir=extracted_package_dir,
            )
```

It seems like it didnt like the old repodata_record.json files that were already there.
but I noticed the error started one package later each time I ran conda create..
the json file was being "repaired" each time it was run later in the _make_single_record function by:

```
if self.is_writable:
     repodata_record = PackageRecord.from_objects(package_cache_record)
     repodata_record_path = join(extracted_package_dir, 'info', 'repodata_record.json')
     try:
         write_as_json_to_file(repodata_record_path, repodata_record)
```

i kind of hackily fixed this by making it try again after it "fixed" the json file by adding:

```
if self.is_writable:
    repodata_record = PackageRecord.from_objects(package_cache_record)
    repodata_record_path = join(extracted_package_dir, 'info', 'repodata_record.json')
    try:
        write_as_json_to_file(repodata_record_path, repodata_record)
        repodata_record = read_repodata_json(extracted_package_dir)
        package_cache_record = PackageCacheRecord.from_objects(
            repodata_record,
            package_tarball_full_path=package_tarball_full_path,
            extracted_package_dir=extracted_package_dir,
        )
        return package_cache_record
```

it took a while to go through every package I had but it worked..

I couldnt see anything wrong with the repodata_record.json files as they were there and looked fine but for some reason they were fixed when I did the above.

removing all the packages would probably also fix the problem - I just didnt want to do that because it would take a while to redownload everything again..

@portig this SSL stuff is a known issue.  We have updated OpenSSL, certifi, and cryptography so that they are current, to maintain the security of our users.  Certifi in particular revoked many certificates, causing the issues you're having.  They did so for the best reason possible: those certificates were no longer secure.  Here is an excerpt from a pending description of the problem and how to fix it:

```
Problems arise when certifi is updated prior to updating openssl and cryptography.  If you are seeing SSL errors, the best way to restore your system is to:

Set ssl_verify to False in .condarc
In .condarc, set the following
ssl_verify: False
Note that this DISABLES verification of SSL certificiates.
Upgrade openssl and cryptography:
conda update openssl cryptography
Update certifi:
conda update certifi

Re-enable SSL verification by removing the ssl_verify line from .condarc
```

I hope this helps get you back on track.

There are several ways to call and API that is not supported by the raw client. First is to manually call the underlying `transport` instance. The only problem there is that you will be responsible for collecting the parameters and constructing the URL, the api is very simple however:

   es = Elasticsearch()
   status, data = es.transport.perform_request('DELETE', 'some_constructed_url', body={"query": {"..."}}, params={"..."})

You will still get most of the benefits of the library:
* connection handling
* (de)serialization
* error handling


Alternatively you can write a plugin for the client itself, you can have a look at https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-watcher-py to see how that'd look.

Hope this helps

You are calling `ESProfile.init()` which should only be done once to create the index - it uploads the settings into the cluster. Looks like you are using an old version of `elasticsearch-dsl` that doesn't have a good detection of whether a settings update is needed so it always tries to update.

The solution is to not call `ESProfile.init()` every time and also upgrade to the latest version of `elasticsearch-dsl`.

I am closing this issue, if problem persists please reopen the issue on the proper repository instead - this is related to `elasticsearch-dsl` and not `elasticsearch-py`. Thank you!

> I understand your situation. Could you please share with me the commands to accomplish the end to end flow?

With this command users create a media account:

`az ams account create -n {amsname} -g {rg} --storage-account {storageAccount} -l {location}`

With this command users create a service principal with {role} access to the media account:

`az ams account sp create -a {amsname} -n {spName} -g {rg} -p {spPassword} --role {role}`

The output of this command provides all the information required to connect to the media account using Azure AD authentication:

```
{
  "AadClientId": "1598b742-a872-421e-a874-2475037db34b",
  "AadEndpoint": "https://login.microsoftonline.com",
  "AadSecret": {spPassword},
  "AadTenantId": "479472dc-cfef-474c-ba12-cba2854af0f6",
  "AccountName": {amsname},
  "ArmAadAudience": "https://management.core.windows.net/",
  "ArmEndpoint": "https://management.azure.com/",
  "Region": {rg-location},
  "ResourceGroup": {rg},
  "SubscriptionId": "d9b02582-2f4f-4b5d-a3dc-ba0015515f7b"
}
```

> Say, I don't see SP list/show/delete command, so wonder a bit for how you can help people find the SP they are interested? 

We only intended to provide a way to easily create and associate a SP with a media account, that's why only the `ams account sp create` and `ams account sp reset-credentials` commands are supported. For additional management, users should use the generic SP commands from the role module.

We are currently providing tests for each command which should fail if there is any breaking change in the SDK dependencies.

Note: In my case, specifying --pre did not work globally because of this (bug?) in pip: https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/4468 . I passed --install-option to control where the scripts are installed and this clobbers --pre specification, resulting in unresolvable packages.

So to clarify: 

BAD: Stable packages are pointing to pre-release package dependencies which are unresolvable. This should be fixed imho.

GOOD: As a workaround, specifying --pre without --install-option makes azure-cli install correctly (it does mean you have to install the pre release version - I tried mixing only pre-release packages for the requirements that demanded so, but that resulted in some commands, like az sql db list backtracing).

To clarify, since version 0.30.0 of the library, the BlobService object was divided into three subservices for the different blob types (BlockBlobService, PageBlobService, AppendBlobService) with common methods in the abstract BaseBlobService that these inherit from.  We did this primarily to reduce confusion when blob-type specific methods were used on the incorrect blob type.

The actual [Blob type](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/ee691964.aspx) you may want to use will depend on your specific use case. To get you started, I'd  recommend checking out  [How to use Azure Blob storage from Python](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/storage-python-how-to-use-blob-storage/) for an overview using the BlockBlobService. Also, the included code samples will give you a much better idea on how to leverage each type of Blob.

Hope this clears things up! :-)

The ClientException is caught at https://github.com/Azure/msrestazure-for-python/blob/master/msrestazure/azure_operation.py#L187 , but then the async code sees the 200 ( at https://github.com/Azure/msrestazure-for-python/blob/master/msrestazure/azure_operation.py#L447 ) and thinks that the operation is done . 

Since deserialization never succeeded, the logic at https://github.com/Azure/msrestazure-for-python/blob/master/msrestazure/azure_operation.py#L460 tries to GET the original request uri, however the import operation is weird and actually doesn't support GET on the URI that you PUT in order to start the operation in the first place.

This is obviously a crazy corner case, so my workaround is to update the operation documentation to allow 200 return code so that this crazy situation doesn't happen.

https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/1263

Please advise if you have any other suggested workaround :)

@asvetlov I've noticed that aiohttp uses the default level for zlib.compressobj, which is 6. I think that is too large for a transport compression. We should set it to 1 (Z_BEST_SPEED). Setting the compress level to Z_BEST_SPEED improves nearly 100% performance for common text data like JSON, with only 2% larger result.

There are a lot of binary data that are already compressed (e.g. JPEG images, MP4 videos, MP3 audio, ZIP archives, etc). Processing such data causes a lot of CPU time, and user should disable compression explicitly (even Z_BEST_SPEED is too slow)

* Our nbconfig system already edits config files in response to REST API calls to the frontend config service.

I just had a look at this again. The optimization I did on the previous round seems to have fixed the URL regex issue completely: it now only takes a few milliseconds to compile.

Of the remaining import duration, 20% is explained by the IPv6 regex compilation. This is easily halved, since right now the compilation is done twice (`IPAddressType` combines v4 and v6).

The majority (> 60%) of the lag comes from importing `urllib`. The only reason it's imported is an obscure feature of `URLType` that can check if a URL is reachable. When I rewrote `URLType` I remember thinking that that doesn't belong in Schematics. Checking if a connection can be established is not a schema validation concern.

So, I would be in favor of removing the feature entirely. (Maybe publish it as a separate recipe.) The alternative is to delay importing `urllib` until a model declares a `URLType` field.

As far as I understand, this is a real bug that occurs when we take the max over an array that can have duplicates of the same variable.  So a simpler function that would have had the same problem is:

```
def f(x):
    return np.max(np.stack([x x]))
```

The same problem also exists for np.maximum(x, x), although I don't know if that's worth fixing.

Anyway, converting a FeatureCollection to a GeometryCollection is a one-way conversion, and I'm trying to avoid cases like that in `geomet`. Here's what you can do:

```python
from geomet import wkt
fc = { ... }  # your FeatureCollection, as a GeoJSON object
geom_wkts = []
for feature in fc['features']:
    geom = feature['geometry']
    geom_wkt = wkt.dumps(geom)
    geom_wkts.append(geom_wkt)

geom_coll = 'GEOMETRYCOLLECTION(%s)' % ','.join(geom_wkts)
```

That should give you what you want.

Thanks so much for your swift response--- and all good then! You have convinced me you know what you are doing.

> A persistent queue and invoking handlers via juju-run is problematic, because the hook environment that caused the particular states to be set and the handler queued will have changed by the time the handler is executed. The states will be making invalid claims about the environment.

I don't think this is really true.  Given an eye toward opening the reactive system up to non-hook sources of change, such as cloud native apps or actions, a handler's conditions might be satisfied when there isn't even a Juju context available, much less a hook context.  And even if there is a hook context, whether that is the same one that the handler eventually executes in is largely irrelevant, except from the point of view of optimizing for latency via fewer contexts on the Juju queue or easing interactive debugging.

I could see an argument to try to take a snapshot of the set of flags set when a given handler is added to the queue, but since there will be plenty of system state that we cannot snapshot, I don't even think that makes sense.  The fact that the handler was triggered is enough to let you know that the conditions that it cares about changed and were such that it made sense for the handler to run again.  We can't stop the overall state of the system from changing again, and we shouldn't try to pretend that it didn't.  It can be useful to know if the state changed back, or if something else handled that condition already; you may want to skip your action in that case, but you may not.  If we (partially) hide the change from you, you can't make an informed decision.

> A helper using juju-run could be installed by the base layer to make this easy for applications ('juju-run python3 -m charms.reactive.main').
> ...
> The next cited issue is handlers not being triggered immediately when state is changed by applications or actions. Using 'juju-run' to trigger the reactive loop seems the solution here for applications, per two paragraphs ago, and actions simply need to trigger the reactive loop themselves since they already have a viable hook context

My point is that the CNA, or action, or whatever shouldn't have to worry about invoking the reactive bus, because the mere existence of the bus is an implementation detail.  Changes to reactive flags should be implicitly reacted to regardless of where they originate.  That is to say, reactive flags are the primary interface.

> The next problem I see is handlers being reinvoked even if their states have not changed. I consider this a feature, and this behaviour is currently being relied on to ensure handlers are invoked every time the hook environment changes.

This is relying on an implementation detail, and goes against the philosophy of reactive.  Hooks are one mechanism by which the charm can be informed of changes to the state of the system, but they shouldn't be the only one.  Specifically, hooks are Juju's way of notifying us of a charm life-cycle event; those events should adjust reactive flags as appropriate and the charm should react to those flag changes.  It is entirely possible that the environment does *not* change just because a hook is invoked, e.g. `update-status`.  I argue that being able to adjust the reactive flags from outside a Juju context and have the handlers do the right thing (a.k.a. push) is better than polling the state of the system periodically with something like `update-status`.

That said, polling can be easier, and I think it would be useful to have the `update-status` hook drive an `update-status` flag; the handler for that flag should poll the status and make any updates, and then remove it.  That enables other sources to inform the charm that the status needs to be updated in a way that prevents duplication and contention over the status messages.

> As an example, a unit in a blocked state will want to keep reinvoking the handler that unblocks it until necessary conditions are met and the reactive state updated.

If the reactive flag is updated, then the handlers that are watching that flag will get called with no need to poll.

> I think @hook vs. states can be solved by not using @hook.

On this, we completely agree.  My proposal is all about taking this all the way, conceptually, and acknowledging that Juju life-cycle events (a.k.a. hooks) are orthogonal to reactive except in that they serve as a source of potential change to the system by setting (and rarely removing) reactive flags.  Beyond that, reactive handlers should fundamentally not assume that they are bound to any particular hook, though we should ensure that they have a Juju context so that they can interact with the Juju agent (I'd like to eventually even remove that requirement, which was basically the idea of cloud native applications, or CNA).

> The first approach in the proposal section seems incompatible with actions.

Not at all.  It simply acknowledges that actions are fundamentally different from Juju life-cycle events but should still be able to inform the reactive system via flags in the same way.

> Actions have a hook context, so the juju-run tasks kicked off by the persistent queue would block until the action exits.

This is merely an optimization to reduce latency and the number of Juju contexts that are used.  I agree that it may be useful to make that optimization, but it may also be that the action might be better served by being decoupled from the handlers that it might trigger.

Reactive handlers are conceptually asynchronous, while actions are conceptually synchronous, or at least imperative.

> This proposal does not address how to handle charm upgrades. Again, the first approach proposed seems to fail here as Juju will be replacing the charm under the persistent queues feet. Inflight handlers will become invalid (as their locations in the .py files has changed, per @only_once problems), or if they are resident in RAM or .pyc then handlers will be trying to access invalid resources on disk such as templates. 

This is a good point that we will certainly have to keep in mind, but I don't think we can avoid it by worrying about which Juju context the handlers are processed in.  Regardless of whether we try for the optimization of depleting the queue within the current Juju context if possible, it is entirely possible for flags to be changed entirely outside of a Juju context, which could cause handlers to be queued which might not be processed until after Juju has updated the charm code.  My point with that possible optimization is to acknowledge that, while the queue needs to exist outside of a Juju context, the worker that processes that queue might not.

However, the queue is really just a list of pending handlers.  That list should be symbolic, and should gracefully handle reactive handlers no longer existing, just like Juju gracefully handles if an upgrade removes a hook that was already queued.  Upgrades would need to cause the handler conditions to be re-evaluated regardless to have new handlers be picked up.

`@only_once` was definitely a mistake and should be removed in favor of the pattern of `@when_not(flag) / flag.set()`.

> In the larger picture, I don't think this gains us anything. I have not come across any problems with charms.reactive that I feel require a backwards incompatible 2.0 release and instead we should be moving forward the existing codebase. The existing design works well and has been pretty much universally adopted because of it.

I appreciate that sentiment, but I have definitely seen complaints about the design and have personally struggled with areas where it would be difficult to make the pattern cleaner and more consistent without a backwards incompatible change.  The coupling of the queue to a single Juju context is one area, and the relation / conversation metaphor is another big one for which I am working on another write-up.

If nothing else, the rename from reactive "states" to reactive "flags" is a breaking change, and I think it affords us a good opportunity to take a hard look at the areas where we can make real improvements.

This project was done as a prototype and it took off faster than anyone expected.  There are definitely areas where that shows.

One idea I've considered is to add a `version` attribute to the `ServerConfig` class. The attribute would contain a value like this:

    ('foreman', '1.7.0')

You could then use `__new__` to customize classes when instances are being created:

```python
class MyEntity(Entity):
    def __new__(cls, server_config=None, **kwargs):
        if server_config.version == ('foreman', '1.7.0'):
            cls.attr = entity_fields.StringField()
        return super(MyEntity, self).__new__(cls, server_config, **kwargs)
```

However, this fails for the simple reason that touching `cls` affects `MyClass`. (Duh…) For example:

```python
>>> class Foo(object):
...   def __new__(cls):
...     cls.bar = 1
...
>>> hasattr(Foo, 'bar')
False
>>> Foo()
>>> hasattr(Foo, 'bar')
True
```

And this would mean that the very definition of `MyClass` is unstable. Why is this an issue? It's an issue because certain classes make use of class attributes when they do their work. For example, here is a snippet from [create_payload](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#nailgun.entity_mixins.EntityCreateMixin.create_payload):

```python
        data = self.get_values().copy()
        api_names = getattr(self.Meta, 'api_names', {})
        for field_name, field in type(self).get_fields().items():
            if field_name in data:
                # mangle `field_name` so that our `field_name`
                # matches what the server expects.
        return data
```

Notice the call to `type`:

    for field_name, field in type(self).get_fields().items():

The class definition is used when determining what data is sent to the server. And if the very definition of the class changes, then the data that is sent to the server will vary over time. Ergh.

Hi Nikolaus,

Thanks for the response, the cma.test examples are really helpful.  Btw, away from the topic, I noticed that in the previous section of the code, in line 255, you had `restricted_gaussian_sampler.GaussVkDSampler`, but I think it should be `GaussVDSampler` since you are testing `VD-CMA`.

I notice that the VkD-CMA takes quite a long time to initialize. For example, a model with 10000 parameters can take close to 5 minutes to initialize with a beefy GCP virtual machine with ~200GB ram and fairly recent cpu cores.  Here is a quick profiling experiment I ran on my machine:

```
import time
import cma
from cma import restricted_gaussian_sampler

def make_es_profiler(num_param):
  start_time = time.time()
  es = cma.CMAEvolutionStrategy( num_param * [1], 1,
                               {
                                'CMA_active': False,
                                'AdaptSigma': None,
                                'CMA_sampler': restricted_gaussian_sampler.GaussVkDSampler,
                                'verbose': -9,
                               })
  end_time = time.time()
  return end_time - start_time

for num in [100, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500, 3000, 4000, 5000, 10000, 11274]:
  print('num param', num, 'time taken (s)', make_es_profiler(num))
```

The results:

```
num param 100 time taken (s) 0.13789987564086914
num param 500 time taken (s) 0.21612834930419922
num param 1000 time taken (s) 0.9570491313934326
num param 1500 time taken (s) 2.02294659614563
num param 2000 time taken (s) 3.6911516189575195
num param 2500 time taken (s) 6.703071594238281
num param 3000 time taken (s) 9.801333904266357
num param 4000 time taken (s) 24.549622535705566
num param 5000 time taken (s) 54.68238401412964
num param 10000 time taken (s) 249.79459977149963
num param 11274 time taken (s) 348.2705271244049
```

I guess the tradeoff in O(N) performance is to to use more memory in the optimizer? In my experiments, where the model has 11274 parameters, using the VkDSampler resulted in much slower performance, I think perhaps despite being O(N), the time constant is fairly large over the learner time constant in front of the O(N^2) default cma settings. The default cma still seems usable at 11274 params, although it is still slow compared to simpler optimizers, so I will still try to use it whenever I can.

I'm also using simpler optimizers, like the variants of PGPE that only adapt sigma to the diagonals to see if the covariance is generally worth it for RL-style tasks.

Thanks again for the help.

```
def check_user_group(authenticated, username, request):
    """authenticated should be the full list from opts. request should be update,list,download

    http://stackoverflow.com/questions/9323834/python-how-to-get-group-ids-of-one-username-like-id-gn"""
    import grp, pwd 
    groups = [g.gr_name for g in grp.getgrall() if username in g.gr_mem]
    gid = pwd.getpwnam(username).pw_gid
    groups.append(grp.getgrgid(gid).gr_name)
    if username in authenticated[request] or [group for group in groups if group in authenticated[request]]:
        return True
    else:
        return False
```

@ewen-naos-nz It's not like I'm unsympathetic to the plight, this is just a hard problem that we've been working to resolve in a satisfactory way. In my opinion the API mistake was creating the `dayfirst` and `yearfirst` options in the first place, but that decision is long past made.

Regarding deployment, I was not suggesting that you somehow pin Ubuntu's version of `python-dateutil`. I have no idea how one would do that. Normally you just pin your dependencies in `setup.py` or `requirements.txt` like this:

```
python-dateutil<2.4.2
```

Then `pip` will never select a version of `dateutil` greater than 2.4.1. See #526 and #536 for examples where I pinned broken upstream dependencies (though in this case `dateutil` just had a change in an undocumented and untested portion of the API, it didn't *break* per se).

I would probably recommend just using one of the many other workarounds for this. Or, if you're really just deciding between exactly two formats, it's almost certainly faster to just try `datetime.strptime` twice.

In the relatively simple situation you describe, I'd probably just not use an RRULE at all:

```python
from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta
from datetime import datetime
from itertools import count

dtstart = datetime(2015, 1, 31)
interval = 1
MONTH = relativedelta(months=1)

rr = (dtstart + x * MONTH for x in count(1, interval))

print([next(rr) for x in range(5)])
```

```
 datetime.datetime(2015, 6, 30, 0, 0)]
```

I would like to offer some temporary solutions for your urgent case. Please explain more how you use migrations. You probably know that migrations are important with django-salesforce only if the same SalesforceModel class is used also for another type of database. People save normal Django data to other databases with `django.db.models.Model` in other directories (apps) and they use the combination of more database backends with one app usually only for easier and faster tests and not for the same persistent data in both databases. (I don't know about such case currently. I use it for some type of backup or cache, but I can create a new complete backup if the model is changed.) Most of them even run tests with sqlite3 in memory and there is really nothing to migrate. They need only to migrate application with normal Django model that use other databases for persistent data. They need only to make the changes in SalesforceModels manually in Salesforce. They run

    python manage.py makemigrations your_other_db_apps_labels ...  # not SF apps

The command "makemigrations" with SF is usually only for a convenience that other apps need not to be listed and that Salesforce migrations will be simply never used then. So, if you still have a migration for Salesforce you can rename temporary the directory `migrations` in your SF app to something different in order to avoid eventual warning (no migrations are better than obsolete migrations)

If your urgent case is that you can not create migrations for normal Django by the command above, please write it explicitly.

Another example of modes usage are shell bindings. But `mode` argument supplied by them is only used for `mode` segment because color changes are simply not defined for every group and color, except the `mode` group itself.

Note that I would not accept change to header because I do not expect people to connect their autocommand which resources vimrc with something described as “any autocommand that changes colorscheme”, or even “any autocommand that runs `:colorscheme` command”: purpose of the autocommand that resources vimrc does not fit into the description and it is very easy to forget that vimrc contains `:colorscheme` call at all. On the opposite side autocommand that changes colorscheme (which has this purpose) cannot be naturally connected with autocommand that resources vimrc.

Not sure whether autocommands that change colorscheme are common enough to include them in troubleshooting (though I do know that you are not the only person who uses per-filetype colorschemes).

Hi @frogg 

I'm glad to hear that you're finding Turi Create helpful for building your AI powered app!  
Have your tried the [activity classification toolkit](https://apple.github.io/turicreate/docs/userguide/activity_classifier/)? It's designed specifically for this kind of tasks.

Let me try to answer all of your questions:
> Is Turi Create optimized for Apple Watch classification?

TC is degined to support any kind of ML task, on a variety of OSes and devices.  
Having said that - the ability to easily create Core ML models makes it a perfect tool for creating powerful Apple Watch, iOS and macOS applications.  
Activity classifcation using Apple Watch and iPhone sensors was of course tested and demonstrated to be an exelnet use case for TC.
> Is it important on which body part the device is located? In the demos I've only seen iPhone-like devices in the pocket.
What if I recorded training data on the left wrist and someone wears their watch on the right wrist?

As in any machine learning model, training on a wide range of scenarios would asisst the model to generalize and get better results.  
It hard to tell how a model would behave before actually testing it - but of course that introducing a completely different scenario on inference, which the model never had the chance to train on, is likely to produce poorer results.
> Which parameters of CMDeviceMotion would be best to feed into Turi Create? Right now I use `.userAcceleration` and `.rotationRate`?

We generally don't tend to advise on project specific details.  
On a general note - some classification tasks would benefit from using calbrated readings, such as `.userAcceleration` and `.rotationRate`, while others would produce better results by using raw sensors readings such as `.CMGyroData`.  
My best advice would be to try both approaches (no point in intermixing them, as they're corelated with each other), and see what works best for your use case.

Feel free to approach with any more questions and clarifications!

Keras model input shape (None, 400) represents (Batch=None, Seq=400),  i.e. the model takes in a sequence scalars of length 400
The CoreML model is not bound to a particular sequence length. It can take any seq. length input. The sequence must of multi arrays which are of size (Double 1), i.e. scalars. 

The following code should clarify this:

```
import numpy as np
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Dense, Embedding, GlobalAveragePooling1D
import coremltools

embedding_dims = 50
max_len = 300

model = Sequential()
model.add(Embedding(2000,
                    embedding_dims,
                    input_length=max_len))
model.add(GlobalAveragePooling1D())
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))
print(model.summary())
print('keras input shape: ', model.input_shape)
print('keras output_shape: ', model.output_shape)

X = np.random.randint(low=0, high=embedding_dims, size=(1,max_len))
keras_out = model.predict(X)

coreml_model = coremltools.converters.keras.convert(model)
input_dict = {}
input_dict['input1'] = np.reshape(X.flatten(), (max_len, 1, 1)).astype(np.float64) #input: [Seq,Batch,Channel]

print('keras out: ', keras_out)
print('coreml out with input seq. 300: ', coreml_model.predict(input_dict)['output1'])
input_dict['input1'] = input_dict['input1'][:5, :, :]
print('coreml out with input seq. 5: ', coreml_model.predict(input_dict)['output1']) 
```

```python
score = np.dot(user_embeddings[i], item_embeddings[j]) + item_biases[j]
```

Hi Victory,

Sorry for delay in response, I will try to answer questions one-by-one, so:

---

> Question: Does it make sense to create providers for sub-parsers (such as `NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser`)?

It mostly depends on 3 things:

1. Are `NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser` used anywhere else? If not, don't create providers for them.
2. Do `NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser` have their own dependencies? If yes, create providers for them.
3. What granularity of structure details you want to see? If you want to see every single component, then create provider for it.

---

> Second question: Should AssetParser object create `NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser` through providers or directly calling constructors?
> But are there any reason that AssetParser should use these providers for NamespacesParser and TransformersParser rather than to call the constructors directly?

`NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser` should have their own providers if:

1. They are used in other places.
2. They have their own dependencies.

---

> Should I dependency-inject `NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser` into `AssetParser`?

Good question. I don't know your architecture, but it seems that you should inject them because they are "important" parts of the `AssetParser`. However, meaning of word "important" can vary from project to project. Objective reasons for using providers are the same:

1. They are used in other places.
2. They have their own dependencies.

I, typically, tend to use providers even in simple cases to follow consistent approach, but I often don't use providers for exceptions.

---

> What's about defining a provider of `NamespacesParser` (in order to easily switch between factory provider and singleton provider), but call the provider directly from `AssetParser` instead of dependency injection (because I find no reason to use dependency injection)?

It's possible, but it will not be dependency injection anymore. I loved this explanation (http://python-dependency-injector.ets-labs.org/introduction/what_is_di.html#best-explanation-ever):

Question:

> How to explain dependency injection to a 5-year-old?

Answer:

> When you go and get things out of the refrigerator for yourself, you can cause problems. You might leave the door open, you might get something Mommy or Daddy doesn’t want you to have. You might even be looking for something we don’t even have or which has expired. **What you should be doing is stating a need**, “I need something to drink with lunch,” **and then** we will **make sure you have something** when you sit down to eat.

---

Making a conclusion,

I would recommend to make a decision on creating or not creating separate providers for `NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser` based on answer to question: are `NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser` "important, sensible" parts of your architecture. If these things are more implementation details and they don't have any external dependencies, then it might have sense to don't create separate providers for them.

I also would recommend to dependency-inject `NamespacesParser` and `TransformersParser` into `AssetParser` not depending on "providers" question. The only exception is that you still treat them like something very minor, like exception.

---

Thanks,
Roman

Hi @arturoribes ,

Sorry for a delay in response, but I'm finally here. First of all, let me say, that your temporary solution looks good and I do like design approach you use.

Regarding of how Dependency Injector can handle it, I think that it might be done without writing extra function following next way:

```python
database = providers.Factory(SqlAlchemyDatabaseService, session=session)

tokens = providers.Factory(
    TokensService,
    id_generator=id_generator,
    database=providers.Factory(database, base_class=Token),
)

users = providers.Factory(
    UsersService,
    id_generator=id_generator,
    database=providers.Factory(database, base_class=User),
)

tokens_service = tokens()
assert tokens_service.database.base_class is Token

users_service = users()
assert users_service.database.base_class is User
```

This is sort of "micro-pattern" of Dependency Injector, I call it "Chained Providers". Main idea of "Chained Providers" is next: `Factory(Factory(..., *args, **kwargs), *args, **kwargs)`. I think that the best explanation here could be provided by following code:

```python
# 1. Keyword arguments of upper level factory are added to lower level factory
chained_dict_factory = providers.Factory(
    providers.Factory(dict, arg1=1),
    arg2=2,
)
print(chained_dict_factory())  # prints: {'arg1': 1, 'arg2': 2}


# 2. Keyword arguments of upper level factory have priority
chained_dict_factory = providers.Factory(
    providers.Factory(dict, arg1=1),
    arg1=2,
)
print(chained_dict_factory())  # prints: {'arg1': 2}

# 3. Keyword arguments provided from context have most priority
chained_dict_factory = providers.Factory(
    providers.Factory(dict, arg1=1),
    arg1=2,
)
print(chained_dict_factory(arg1=3))  # prints: {'arg1': 3}
```

Hope it helps and thanks for the question,
Roman

@kirschju 

A few thoughts:

* I was going to suggest you try a debug build but taking a closer look I think you'll run into problems.
 It looks like in ``small_object_allocator::allocate(size_t size)`` if you do a debug build (has ``Z3DEBUG`` defined) and aren't on windows it simply returns `` memory::allocate(size)`` (and similarly in ``small_object_allocator::dellocate()``). This means if there's a bug in the ``small_object_allocator`` you might miss it in a debug build. 

* The ``SASSERT()`` calls later on in the code of  ``small_object_allocator::allocate(size_t size)`` are no-ops in release builds which (due to the early return in a debug build on non-windows platforms) means these asserts are **never being checked** on non-windows builds of Z3! @NikolajBjorner do you agree with this assessment? Having those ``SASSERT()``s never checked on non windows builds does not seem desirable to me.

My suggestion would be to remove the early return in  ``small_object_allocator::allocate(size_t size)`` and  ``small_object_allocator::deallocate(size_t size)`` and then do a debug build to see if any of those ``SASSERT()`` calls fire in your use case.

If that doesn't work you could also try building with the Address Sanitizer. You just need a recent GCC or Clang and build like

```
CXX=clang++ CC=clang CXXFLAGS="-fno-omit-frame-pointer -fsanitize=address" LDFLAGS="-fsanitize=address" python scripts/mk_make.py --build build_asan --noomp
cd build_asan
make -j4
```
Make sure you use a fresh build directory (i.e. build_asan should be empty or not exist) because the build system messes up if you try to do a build in a directory that has already been used for a build.

Note there are other unresolved problems build Z3 with the Address Sanitizer (#436) so you may run into problems.

> nolearn.lasagne has net.initialize() that does the same thing as compile but it's implicitly called during fit() if it hasn't been called by the user before.

But a difference I guess is that you specify certain parameters when calling `compile()`, which nolearn expects to receive in `__init__()`. It probably makes more sense to group everything together, although there is something to be said for splitting up long argument lists into multiple steps, it might make things easier to remember because of the 'hierarchical' structure. But it does seem a bit arbitrary.

> nolearn.lasagne (and I think Keras too) allow you to pass a mapping of {layer_name: input_value} as X. Multiple outputs aren't supported yet, but it's something on my list.

Awesome!

> The use case for no labels is unsupervised training? When I train auto-encoders, I will usually just pass the same thing (maybe transposed) as X and y.

In some cases it might be limiting to treat labels as a special type of input. Most tasks fit the (X, y)-paradigm (i.e. data + labels), but there are many that don't. I'd be okay with having features that are specific to this paradigm, because it is so common. But there are are many multiple input / multiple output situations as well, e.g. training siamese networks with pairwise / triplet / quadruplet losses, models with example weights, various forms of masking, ... so I think we should have something generic that can handle all of it. 

> nolearn.lasagne has net.score(X, y), which is the same thing but the name's from scikit-learn Again Keras uses a different method name for no reason apparently.

It's beginning to look like I need to take a more thorough look at the nolearn docs :)

I'm not a huge fan of callbacks myself, I feel that iterators / generators are a much more natural way of passing control back and forth. But perhaps we can accommodate both styles.

> Batch normalization could be a candidate as well -- if we integrate cuDNN v5's interface in Theano, we may want to change the default interface for the BatchNormLayer (i.e., have it remember the variance instead of the inverse standard deviation).

The cuDNN v5 interface is a bit weird in this respect -- it still returns the inverse standard deviation during the forward pass to be reused in the backward pass, but it also allows to update the non-inverted standard deviation, and the inference implementation requires the non-inverted standard deviation. A possible solution would be to use cuDNN just to obtain the batch-normalized data, its mean and its inverse standard deviation in one go (and also use cuDNN for the backward pass), but handle inference and the exponential moving average on our own. I've implemented the necessary Ops for Theano (Theano/Theano/pull/4582). We can only integrate this in Lasagne past v0.2 if we still want v0.2 to be compatible with Theano 0.8, but it means we can keep the BatchNormLayer interface as it is.

```Python
if not masked:
            running_mean.default_update = ((1 - self.alpha) * running_mean +
                                           self.alpha * input_mean)
            running_var.default_update = ((1 - self.alpha) * running_var +
                                          self.alpha * input_var)
else:   # dont change running stats when masked
            running_mean.default_update = (
                       mask*((1 - self.alpha) * running_mean + self.alpha * input_mean)  + 
                       (1-mask)*running_mean)
            running_var.default_update = (
                       mask*((1 - self.alpha) * running_var + self.alpha * input_var) + 
                       (1-mask)*running_var )
```

One thing that I think we could do is change the way the results are stored. At the moment we calculate the diff as part of the import and save it in the `row_result`. In my case, with many foreign keys, calculating the diff is expensive. It also results in the html embedded in the diff.

For an emailed result, I guess that we would control over the formatting at the output stage. I.e. some people might the full results as html, others might want a summary, and others still might want a CSV of the results.

If we change the `import_data` so that it stores the old and new instances in the `row_result` and then change `diff` to a property so that it returns the current formatting, but calculated at the time it is asked for, then we defer processing (which might never be called - I do batch imports and never show the diff) and provide flexibility for other people to do custom reporting. I don't know whether we need to worry about the memory implications of keeping two copies of each object around until the import finishes. If we decide to go ahead, I would be happy to make those changes as part of #346

# The Bug

The simplest way to demonstrate this bug is:
```python
from Ganga.GPIDev.Lib.GangaList.GangaList import GangaList as GL
gl = GL()
gl == gl  # This should be True and is
gl != gl  # This should be False but is True!
```
This is happening because `GangaList.__ne__` has a check for `LHS is RHS` which, if `True`, returns `True`. This is incorrect and a bug.

# The hiding of the bug

The reason we have not seen this bug in the test suite etc. is that we are usually dealing with proxied `GangaList`s. 
```python
glp = GangaList()  # A proxied GangaList
glp == glp  # Should be True and is
glp != glp  # Should be False and is
```
What is happening here is that `glp != glp` goes though a number of steps before it gets to `__ne__`:

1. `glp != glp`
2. `Ganga.GPIDev.Base.Proxy.GangaList.__ne__(glp, glp)` then via `ProxyMethodDescriptor` goes to
3. `Ganga.GPIDev.Lib.GangaList.GangaList.__ne__(stripProxy(glp), glp)`

Since `stripProxy(glp) is not glp`, the incorrect (with `self` = `stripProxy(glp)` and `obj_list` = `glp`):
```python
if obj_list is self:
    return True
```
is not used and so it defaults back to the usual (and correct):
```python
self._list.__ne__(self.strip_proxy_list(obj_list))
```
which correctly returns `False`.

It looks like #102 has fixed this bug. I'm still getting some problems with the jobs.metadata registry but I believe these are caused by the test framework rather than anything else.

The thing is that, for the example of Executable, `'args'` is not in the `__dict__` of an instance of `Executable` but rather in the `__dict__` of the class itself. For example you can do:
```python
class Foo(object):
  a = 'something'

o = Foo()

print Foo.__dict__
print o.__dict__
print o.a
```
gives
```python
{'a': 1, '__dict__': <attribute '__dict__' of 'foo' objects>, '__module__': '__main__', '__weakref__': <attribute '__weakref__' of 'foo' objects>, '__doc__': None}
{}
something
```
In our case we have, in the class's `Executable.__dict__` the descriptor objects which are given an *instance* of the class as `obj` and it extracts the values from `obj._data`. This works because all `Executable` objects have the same set of attributes, even if the *values* of those attributes are different.

> how would we find the session /credentials to use to authenticate the request?

The session won't be tied to the account record but rather stored in the cache backend.
We will use a hmac of the hawk session ID to store the hawk secret and the user ID.

When we receive the Hawk Authorization header we will have the ID in the header.
We can then hmac it to find the key in the cache backend.

> Do you think we should save the sessions as their own independent records and then also save session tokens on account records when we first create sessions? 

Yes we should save session as their own indepedent records (I don't think it is necessary to store them in the storage backend) I guess if we store the hawk secret, hawk algorithm and user ID in the session record we will have the mandatory information to link it to the user.

```
Hello,

We are nearly ready to release PyWavelets v0.4.0.  Please test from current master and report any problems you encounter (https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/).  The current plan is to tag the release on Dec 28.

I have pasted a summary of the changes from our current draft of the release notes below.  There are several new features and performance improvements in addition to some API cleanup.



New features
============

1D and 2D stationary wavelet transforms
---------------------------------------
1D (``iswt``) and 2D (``iswt2``) stationary wavelet transforms were added.
These currently only support even length inputs


Faster 2D and nD wavelet transforms
-----------------------------------
The multidimensional DWT and IDWT code was refactored and is now an order of
magnitude faster than in previous releases.  The following functions benefit:
``dwt2``, ``idwt2``, ``dwtn``, ``idwtn``.


Complex floating point support
------------------------------
64 and 128-bit complex data types are now supported by all wavelet transforms.


nD implementation of the multilevel DWT and IDWT
------------------------------------------------
The existing 1D and 2D multilevel transforms were supplemented with an nD
implementation.


Wavelet transforms can be applied along a specific axis/axes
------------------------------------------------------------
All wavelet transform functions now support explicit specification of the axis
or axes upon which to perform the transform.


Example Datasets
----------------
Two additional 2D grayscale images were added (`camera`, `ascent`).  The
previously existing 1D ECG data (`ecg`) and the 2D aerial image (`aero`)
used in the demos can also now be imported via functions defined in
`pywt.data` (e.g. ``camera = pywt.data.camera()``)


Deprecated features
===================

A number of functions have been renamed, the old names are deprecated and will
be removed in a future release:

- ``intwave``, renamed to ``integrate_wavelet``
- ``centrfrq``, renamed to ``central_frequency``
- ``scal2frq``, renamed to ``scale2frequency``
- ``orthfilt``, renamed to ``orthogonal_filter_bank``

Integration of general signals (i.e. not wavelets) with ``integrate_wavelet``
is deprecated.

The ``MODES`` object and its attributes are deprecated.  The new name is
``Modes``, and the attribute names are expanded:

- ``zpd``, renamed to ``zero``
- ``cpd``, renamed to ``constant``
- ``sp1``, renamed to ``smooth``
- ``sym``, renamed to ``symmetric``
- ``ppd``, renamed to ``periodic``
- ``per``, renamed to ``periodization``

Backwards incompatible changes
==============================

``idwt`` no longer takes a ``correct_size`` parameter. As a consequence,
``idwt2`` inputs must match exactly in length. For multilevel transforms, where
arrays differing in size by one element may be produced, use the ``waverec``
functions from the ``multilevel`` module instead.

Bugs Fixed
==========

float32 inputs were not always respected. All transforms now return float32
outputs when called using float32 inputs.

Incorrect detail coefficients were returned by `downcoef` when `level > 1`.

Other changes
=============

Much of the API documentation is now autogenerated from the corresponding
function docstrings.  The numpydoc sphinx extension is now needed to build the
documentation.
```

I am afraid "not counting" the bans isn't a good way to approach this problem.

We need to remember that `crawlera` is a different service than `scrapy`. Crawlera effectively returns bad statuses (503 in this case) when it is being hit by a lot of requests knowing that it was already banned.

The best approach here would be to slow down the requests `scrapy` is doing to the service, and this is totally configurable for the user (building a spider to handle such cases).

It would be very good for example if crawlera could return a header with the information on when to hit it again, specifically when using crawlera sessions, but sadly, that isn't the case yet.
@eLRuLL see also issue #39687 for Crawlera
Using a CNAME record at the apex breaks RFC, but we do have an ALIAS record which behaves like a CNAME and does some pre-fetching to return an IP as expected instead. This will work well for you.

SetupAPI section
----------------
- Use SETUP API to enumerate all Camera devices
