Direct HUB section
------------------
https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/hardware/ff537421(v=vs.85).aspx

Loop and open each HDC[x] file which represent a HUB.
For each CreateFile that succeed call IOCTL : USB_ROOT_HUB_NAME
- CreateFile the RootHubName retrieved
- Call: IOCTL_USB_GET_NODE_INFORMATION on the returned hub to retrieve the hub and retrieve the number of available ports. If the node is a Hub we need to call the internal hub as well.
- For each port:
-- Call IOCTL_USB_GET_NODE_CONNECTION_INFORMATION_EX to make sure the device is connected (by looking at the ConnectionStatus flag). 
-- If connected, call IOCTL_USB_GET_NODE_CONNECTION_DRIVERKEY_NAME to fetch the driver key of the device.

> could you explain how it works?

The key is to notice that the ReplayBuffer gets whatever observations came out of `env.step`. When using LazyFrames, this is just a list of references instead of whole numpy arrays. The actual arrays are stored in the heap and the replay buffer is filled with lists of pointers.
Thus, when you ask the replay buffer for a sample, it actually returns a collection of LazyFrames!

>> one of the most critically useful features of Graphene
> In any event, I am curious what you are doing that makes this so useful.

The answer here is twofold: one for my particular use case, and one for why *in general* it's useful. The reason I think it's useful in general is that it means that you can continue to reuse whatever your data model type is, instead of having to reassign useful data to your graphene `ObjectType` instances.

In my case, it means that I can just pass around Django `Model` instances, rather than having to populate my `ObjectType`. This is especially important because Django does semi-lazy lookups and queries based on attribute access, so it's incredibly useful to have graphene look up only the desired attributes. My code is here: https://github.com/Lucretiel/SkillServe/blob/v2018/skillboards/graphql/query.py

Notice, for example, in [this snippet](https://github.com/Lucretiel/SkillServe/blob/4dbe0d782501f66f06a048c472c414f2a3776159/skillboards/graphql/query.py#L56-L79). I `resolve_games` returns a Django `QuerySet`, which is unresolved until graphql actually iterates over it to get the individual database rows. The objects of the QuerySet are, themselves, not graphql objects, but `Model` instances. GraphQL knows from the schema that `Player.games` is a list of [`schema.Game`](https://github.com/Lucretiel/SkillServe/blob/v2018/skillboards/graphql/query.py#L82-L92), and automatically applies that schema to the `models.Game` instance.

It sounds complicated but it really makes sense once you're used to it.

In my original bug posting, I mentioned that the documents hint at this functionality. That hinting happens [here](http://docs.graphene-python.org/en/latest/types/objecttypes/#resolvers):

> NOTE: The resolvers on a ObjectType are always treated as staticmethods, so the first argument to the resolver method self (or root) need not be an actual instance of the ObjectType.

Note that default TF DNNs cannot support monotonicity, so even if you impose monotonicity in the calibrators, it does not guarantee monotonicity in the final function composed of calibration and DNN. If you still want to impose monotonicity only for the calibrators you can do one of the following:

To applying the projction:

(A) If you are *not* using tf.learn and estimator api, you can do something like this:

```
(y, projection_op, _) = tfl.calibration_layer(...)
loss = ...
train_op = ...
for _ in range(1000):
    batch_xs, batch_ys = ...
    # Apply gradient update.
    sess.run(train_op, feed_dict={x: batch_xs, y: batch_ys})
    # Apply projeciton op (can be done once per batch or less often).
    sess.run(projection_op)
```
(B) If you are using the estimator API, you can create an estimator by inheriting from tfl.Calibrated. E.g. take tensorflow_lattice/python/estimators/calibrated_linear.py and replace prediction_builder_from_calibrated with a DNN instead of the linear combination.

In the coming days we will be pushing a few updates to the estimator library to make (‌‌‌B) simpler and easier to extend. We will try to add an example with monotonicity constraints with that update.

One use case is coding for an NCP host, using code that can be recompiled to use Openthread on an SOC system.  What I mean by that is in one instance a firmware application would be resident on the same processor as Openthread and would interact with it through a middle layer module which hides whether Openthread is on the same processor (SOC) or a different processor (NCP).  The most efficient implementation for this application is to use Openthread types for all of the interactions between it and Openthread whether or not it is NCP or SOC.  The challenge is in identifying what subset of files must reside on the host in the NCP model in order for the system to build.  Currently, that set is limited to the following;
- types.h
- toolchain.h <required by types.h>
- code_utils.hpp <required by hdlc.cpp>
- spinel.c <provides api's for packing/unpacking UART frames>
- spinel.h
- hdlc.cpp <provides api's for  encoding/decoding UART frames>
- hdlc.hpp

The fewer files the better, as every time the system adopts a new firmware image for the NCP it must also adopt a new set of source files for what represents the host side footprint. Currently there are two files; toolchain.h and code_utils.hpp, that are brought in to satisfy other required files. If more header files are required, it is only reasonable to assume the list of indirect requirements would also increase.

For ease of supporting NCP hosts, I would ask that we make the set of required source files that a host must adopt remain as small as possible.  This of course means that I would like all of the openthread types to reside in a single types file with as few as possible #include directives inside.

It would also be useful if the set of files needed to build a NCP host in this fashion were pre-packaged into a directory either as part of make or perhaps some other operation.

Ok, I see where you are coming from.

I'd rather not make any assumptions about the version string, other than mandating that it be represented in the firmware image as a continuous string. Trying to parse it to extract the date is likely to be error-prone.

Now that I understand what you are trying to accomplish, here is my new proposal:

Let's rename the function from `otGetPlatformVersion()` to `otGetVersionString()` and move it out of the platform and into OpenThread. Then let's modify the function to include the ability to add things to the version string at build time:

```
const char *otGetVersionString(void)
{
    static const char sPlatformVersion[] =
        PACKAGE_NAME "/" PACKAGE_VERSION "; "
#ifdef OT_EXTRA_VERSION_INFO
        OT_EXTRA_VERSION_INFO "; "
#endif
        __DATE__ " " __TIME__;

    return sPlatformVersion;
}
```

We then allow `OT_EXTRA_VERSION_INFO` to be set by the user via a `configure` command-line argument. This would allow builds with external platforms to specify any additional build information directly into the version string in a way that produces a single coherent string in firmware.

If `OT_EXTRA_VERSION_INFO` is not specified on the command line, then it is either left undefined or set to `OPENTHREAD_EXAMPLES` if that is set.

For example, with the above mechanism implemented, the marble build system would call `configure` with something like this:

```
./configure --with-extra-version-info="Marble/`git describe --always`"
```

def load_vgg_simple(graph_def_path, model_path):

    # Load the network
    with gfile.FastGFile(graph_def_path, 'rb') as f:
        graph_def = tf.GraphDef()
        graph_def.ParseFromString(f.read()) # load graph
    graph = tf.Graph()
    graph.as_default()
    tf.import_graph_def(graph_def, name='')  # set default
    with tf.Session(graph=graph) as persisted_sess:
        network = vgg16(is_test=True)
        model = tflearn.DNN(network, session = persisted_sess)
        model.load(model_path, weights_only=True)
    return model.session

@TheNavigat Thanks for looking into this. It looks like there's a huge complexity wall here. The robustness of parsing everything may be offset by the sheer complexity and overhead of maintaining the resulting codebase. How far can we get without writing a full parser+processor for m4 in Python? Can we do something simple to catch at least 90% of configuration setups? For the final 10% of use cases, we'd need to focus on detecting the potential failure, and either warning or prompting the user before continuing with further action-- can we do that reliably, too?

So, two main questions to mull over:
1. How many use cases do we catch by simply rolling our own version of `starttls.m4`, then dropping `include(starttls.m4)` into `sendmail.mc`?
2. How reliably can we guess that (1) will fail in some way?

And some side questions to think about later:
 -  What are the cases where a single Sendmail instance might want to install multiple certificates, and which of these cases should we handle?  Might someone want to host a submission server on a separate hostname from the MTA? Might someone want to install a cert corresponding with their mail domain(s) and not their hostname?
     - Background: any certificate installation via Certbot also knows about the `domain` for which that certificate is being issued. For HTTP servers, this is used to determine which "virtual host" to install the certificate on. For MTAs, this notion doesn't make as much sense. MTA hostnames and IPs are typically mapped 1-1-- most MTAs don't support SNI for that reason. However, sendmail does have a `DOMAIN` macro to specify per-domain parameters, but these seem to correspond with the mail domain (the part after the @ in an email address) and not the server's hostname.
 - And lastly, I'm curious whether other distros ship any additions to sendmail.

Hopefully the above was somewhat helpful? Let me know if you have any questions about the above. I won't have the bandwidth to work directly on this for a month or so, but am happy to continue investigating things like common configuration setups, usage edge cases, etc. Will let you know here if I learn anything relevant.

I don't feel one way or another too strongly, but my preference is to not include output like this. The only reason I can think of where a user should be using `--force-renew` is if they want to change a property of their cert (such as the key size) and don't want to answer the following interactive prompt:
```
What would you like to do?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: Keep the existing certificate for now
2: Renew & replace the cert (limit ~5 per 7 days)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Select the appropriate number [1-2] then [enter] (press 'c' to cancel):
```
Including `--force-renewal` in standard renewal output suggests this flag to many more people than that.

My personal preference on how to fix this problem is to make the expiration notice sent by Let's Encrypt as clear as possible so users don't think there's a problem with their cert's renewal when there isn't one.

Eduard Braun:
> @nthykier Might make sense... When I first started working on Scour (probably sometime when Python 3.3 was current) but nowadays the difference seems significant (I hope we did not regress Python 2.7 performance at some point...).
> 

I believe that most of the performance difference are due to changes in
python3 itself (without any tests to back this claim).  I had a look at
the release notes for python 3.4 - 3.6 and there I found:

 * The property() getter calls are up to 25% faster. (Contributed by Joe
   Jevnik in bpo-23910.) [py3.5]

   - This affects many of our calls to the DOM.  A method like
     getAttributeValue, which is the "top 2 in total time" for
     "example.svg" in LP#490197 (imported as GH#77), uses a
     property()-generated getter on the underlying Attr object
     (and it is not the only affected method).

 * The Python interpreter now uses a 16-bit wordcode instead of bytecode
   which made a number of opcode optimizations possible. (Contributed by
   Demur Rumed with input and reviews from Serhiy Storchaka and Victor
   Stinner in bpo-26647 and bpo-28050.) [py3.6]

   - This is just a general speed improvement all around.

Note that in my tests, I have used python 3.6 for python3.  It would be
interesting to see how python 3.3 compares (but I do not have that
installed).

Thanks,
~Niels

[py3.5]: https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/3.5.html#optimizations

[py3.6]: https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/3.6.html#optimizations

try 
```
which -a python
which -a pip
```
The first entry of the return is the path that is called if you type python/pip in your terminal (assuming you use Mac) 
Then check if your pip put tpot in the right directory. The right directory is referred to in your python under $sys.path. You can check your sys.path this way:

```
import sys
print("\n".join(sys.path))
```


Also check that your .bash_profile (or .zshrc as in my case) contains
```
export PYTHONPATH=$PYTHONPATH:/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages
```
while the path after `$PYTHONPATH:` should be where tpot is. To find your tpot, start each installed python and check each sys.path returns ending with site-packages for tpot. 

If it still doesn't work try to append your tpot path to $sys.path of your preferred python.

I like the general idea of the proposal. Extending `ray.get` support to data structures beyond lists seems natural. In addition, I think that it could make sense to support generators too.

I am concerned about about consistency with the API. For example, claiming support for `ndarrays`, but only when `n=1` seems restrictive. On the other hand, support for `n>1` would imply support for nested data structures (e.g. nested lists) which could quickly complicate and introduce bugs to `ray.get`.

Furthermore, why add explicit support for ndarrays which already implement `tolist()`? From my understanding, computation time, object store time, and deserialization time are far greater than the time spent calling `tolist()`.

(D) might be good. It certainly resolves problem (2).

Possible disadvantages of (D):

1. Suppose `f` is a remote function and we want to add in the ability to call `f` locally in a blocking manner. Then it isn't clear how to do this if we do (D). That said, there are easy workarounds, since the user can just define `f` without the decorator, and then define `f_remote = ray.remote(f)`, so they could call `f` for the blocking version and `f_remote` for the non-blocking version.

2. Currently, if a user is parallelizing an existing code base, and they decorate a function `f` with `@ray.remote`, then all existing invocations of `f()` will automatically raise exceptions, so the user will immediately be made aware of which parts of the code they need to change. This is pretty useful I think. I suppose this is also an argument for changing actor method invocations.

3. Invoking functions with `.remote` potentially makes the code more readable.

Note that there is a somewhat fundamental asymmetry between remote functions and actor methods. That is, remote functions could in principle be executed remotely or locally in the same process. However, actor methods can *only* be executed remotely on the worker process that actually contains the actor state.

Yes, that Reddit post (which I hadn't seen – thanks) describes it well. The third way is the one I used here, since `sphinx-argparse` expects to find a function, as described above.

So, after this PR is merged, the `maketheparser()` function will already be in place.

Then, to fully replace **Getopt** with **Argparse**, someone needs to make the following changes in `main()`:

1. Remove the function `usage()` (Argparse generates this automatically).
2. Remove the block `try: / except:` (Argparse also manages exceptions and error mesages automatically)
3. Replace those two blocks , at the top of `main()`, with:
```
    P = maketheparser()
    A = P.parse_args(args=args)
```
4. Leave the variable definitions as they are for now (I think – some are setting up default values, so Argparse may already be taking care of those).
5. Replace the block `for(k, v) in opts:` with a series of `if` statements. Instead of checking the value of `k` for the condition, they should check the equivalent Argparse options from the parser object (`A.page_numbers`, etc.). For this you can follow the example of `pdf2txt.py`, which is working correctly.

I hope that's helpful.

For the record, the reason I didn't do this in the first place is that I feel like `shield` should be a pretty obscure and rarely used feature. It's​ very easy to use in a way that breaks your program in subtle ways (e.g. by making it unresponsive to rare network failure conditions), it breaks the rule that timeout policy should be set as far up the stack as possible, and I don't want it to become an attractive nuisance. So I included it in `open_cancel_scope` since that's a kind of low level "raw" API, but left it out of the high level timeout apis.

Maybe it will turn out the I'm wrong and `shield` really is something that gets used a lot, but I can't help but feel that that would indicate that we've messed up somehow.

In the mean time, I'm sort of feeling like maybe we should remove the kwarg from `open_cancel_scope` :-). It *should* be a bit annoying to use...

While we're at it, here's another idea: maybe we can have a way to say "yes, I know I'm cancelled, but please stop raising it unless an operation *actually* blocks". Like a shield with a zero second per operation timeout. So you could attempt to do things like send a goodbye message, but if it doesn't succeed immediately, oh well, let's move on. It would at least remove the issue of having to pick some magic timeout value way down inside the code...

... Though, I guess this doesn't actually help for your use case with websockets, since the main reason websockets have a goodbye message is so the initiator can then wait until they see the corresponding goodbye message from their peer, and know that both sides successfully processed all the data that was sent. (Since otherwise TCP can lose some data at the end of a connection.) Sending a goodbye message and then immediately closing the socket is pretty useless, but that's all my idea above would allow for.

Otoh I'm not entirely sure I buy that it makes sense to send websocket goodbye messages on control-C in the first place :-). "Potential data loss" sounds bad, but here it just means that the connection might act like you hit control-C a little earlier than you actually did... and who's to say that's not what happened? (Unless you're carefully monitoring the connection state and choosing when to hit control-C with millisecond precision, which seems like a weird hobby.) But maybe the problem is more general – certainly there are a lot of protocols and applications that have some concept of a graceful shutdown.

Here's my current understanding of this.

The problem being fixed is: suppose we have some code like:
```python
try:
    with my_context_manager():
        raise StopIteration
except StopIteration:
    print("caught it!")
```
this is totally fine and normal, and should print `caught it!` (unless the context manager specifically catches this exception, but let's assume it doesn't). Now let's assume `my_context_manager()` is implemented as a `contextlib.contextmanager` wrapper around a generator. Then the way this code should work is that `StopIteration` will be thrown into the generator, which lets it escape... and then we have a problem! PEP 479 made it so that if a generator raises `StopIteration`, this gets automatically converted into a `RuntimeError`. In general this is a good thing because it lets us tell the difference between a generator that raises `StopIteration`, and one that exits normally (which causes the interpreter to automatically raise `StopIteration`). But it makes life complicated for `contextlib.contextmanager`, because if it lets this `RuntimeError` escape, then it'll give the wrong answer on our code above.

So the solution is that the `contextmanager` code needs to detect when we've got a `RuntimeError` that was wrapped around a `StopIteration`, and unwrap it again. And that's what the version I originally copied this code from tried to do. However, it was a bit over-eager in how it did this: it unwrapped *any* `RuntimeError` that it saw that had a `__cause__` attribute, not just `RuntimeError`s that were automatically wrapped around a `StopIteration`. So the idea of the patch we're trying to adapt, is that it makes this check more careful. The two tests are: (a) now if we get a `RuntimeError` that's wrapped around something that's not `StopIteration`, then the `RuntimeError` should be preserved, and (b) if we *do* get a `RuntimeError` that's wrapped around a `StopIteration`, then it should continue to be correctly unwrapped.

Phew, ok, now we understand what's going on upstream with regular synchronous `contextmanager`. How does this translate to our async `acontextmanager`? This is complicated because internally async generators use both `StopIteration` (to signal the end of each call to `anext`/`asend`/`athrow`) and also `StopAsyncIteration` (to signal the end of the overall iteration).

I don't think this is documented anywhere, but based on some experimentation, it looks like the way PEP 479 was applied to the native async generators in 3.6 is: if they raise *either* `StopIteration` *or* `StopAsyncIteration`, then that gets wrapped into a `RuntimeError`.

So this suggests that `acontextmanager`'s unwrapping code needs to check for both of these special exception types, and indeed, [that's exactly what the upstream `asynccontextmanager` (only available in 3.7-dev builds) does](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/6a98a04e21fc3209b2170c3930ce4488e89680e3/Lib/contextlib.py#L175).

Then there's an additional wrinkle: it looks like currently, `async_generator` async generators correctly wrap `StopIteration` in `RuntimeError`, but they *don't* correctly wrap `StopAsyncIteration` in `RuntimeError` – they just let `StopAsyncIteration` pass through unchanged.

So:

We should make the unwrapping logic check for both `StopIteration` and `StopAsyncIteration`, like the upstream `asynccontextmanager` does.

We should probably test throwing both `StopIteration` and `StopAsyncIteration` through an async context manager (like the last test in here does currently).

From comments above, it sounds like making that last test use `StopIteration` doesn't actually work? If so then I'm confused – it seems like it should work to me!

And currently that last test isn't actually testing the unwrapping logic, because it's using `StopAsyncIteration` + an `async_generator`-based generator, so the exception is never getting wrapped in the first place. I'm looking at making `async_generator` properly implement PEP 479, but I'm currently hitting some weird error that I don't understand.

Arguably this is not a big deal though because `acontextmanager` is not a public API, and all of our internal users use it with `async_generator`-based generators, so as long as those two working together give the right result it doesn't really matter why.

I'm glad you asked! 😄 When working on that bug, I made some assumptions that proved wrong, but I did not revisit my decision to keep the test root CA. Let's try to justify this decision:

* I would like to make the change in urllib3 as minimal as possible by changing only the intermediate CA
* The test root CA can't change, because it signed other certificates, so if I want to use another test root CA here, I need to keep the two test root CAs in urllib3, which would be confusing? And how would I name this new root CA?
* I can't generate the CA on the fly in tests until Python 2.6 support is removed in urllib3
* It looks like importing an existing CA in trustme can be generally useful for projects where the generated certs are stored in git: it avoids changing the root CA for every change

The from_pem method above would be quite simple and would mostly be about calling load_pem_x509_certificate and load_pem_private_key, but I realize it would also extend the number of things that need to be maintained in trustme.

The n-step dqn implementation seems incorrect. The point of n-step is to look ahead and use future rewards to better approximate future discounted rewards. In this line ` Rs = (reward_batch **(self.n_step)) + discounted_reward_batch` you raise the reward to n and this is a misinterpretation of the equation for n-step dqn. The exponent is not an actual exponent in the origial algorithm, it is a notation. See this link for example: http://www.incompleteideas.net/book/7/node2.html

To implement n-step we would need to look ahead at future rewards up to n-steps in the future. This can easily be solved by sampling between the first index in the ringbuffers up to the max length - n. When there's a new episode there's no need to look further ahead and you can simply use those or that specific rewards(s) as the target.

> On the Frame/Message renaming.  I think of Framing as being a lower-level construct (down towards the wire protocol) than a Message (which is the application level construct).  I think the *best*way of distinguishing messages and multipart messages is the following terminology:

* Message - the single message version
* Message Group - A group a Messages that are send/recv'd together.

This is simply inaccurate - Message Group would suggest that it is multiple messages somehow associated, rather than a single message with multiple segments.  A *Message* is a single zeromq communication, which may have one or more parts.  That's why it's confusing that pyzmq's Message class *does not* reflect a message, but a *part* of a message.  It is already the convention of other bindings and the Guide that these individual message parts are referred to as Frames.
